content:
How small scripts, boring problems, and browser-based tools turned into steady side income

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For a long time, I thought the only way to make real money from code was to build something big — a SaaS product, a mobile app, something with a pitch deck. So I kept planning, kept sketching architecture diagrams, and kept shipping nothing.
Meanwhile, I was writing small JavaScript tools every week just to solve annoying problems for myself and a few freelancer friends. Form validators. Link checkers. Quick converters. Little browser scripts nobody would call a “startup.”
That’s when it clicked: nobody pays for your ambition. They pay for the ten minutes you just saved them.
Here are 10 small JavaScript tools I built, what they earned, and why none of them needed to be impressive to work.
1. A Link-in-Bio Page Builder That Made My First $40
Creators kept asking friends to “just make me a simple link page.” I built a no-database version: fill a form, get a static HTML page you can host anywhere.
